title: Train a stable-subset expert using adversarial-validation feature pruning, not just weighting
summary: You already use adversarial weights, but covariate-shift literature warns that weighting alone can be high-variance when shift is large.
rationale: You already use adversarial weights, but covariate-shift literature warns that weighting alone can be high-variance when shift is large. Stable-learning work suggests part of the gain can come from identifying a minimal stable variable set, so a second expert trained on low-drift features/interactions may generalize better than the full feature space.
implementation_hint: Run adversarial validation with train vs test labels, score feature importances/SHAP, and split features into stable vs unstable groups. Train one model on the full set with current weights and a second on only stable features plus handpicked robust interactions; blend them by OOF robustness under forward/grouped splits. Also test clipping or removing especially transductive category-frequency features if they dominate drift.
expected_effect: May improve public-LB transfer even if local CV changes little, because it targets distribution mismatch directly rather than maximizing in-fold AUC.
risk: Too aggressive pruning can throw away genuinely predictive race-specific signal and lower CV. This is more likely to help leaderboard robustness than headline local score.
